summary.summary_250: The essay argues that energy retrofits succeed only when landlords and tenants share incentives.
summary.summary_1000: The essay argues that energy retrofits succeed only when landlords and tenants share incentives, because split ownership weakens adoption.
summary.tldr: Energy retrofits need aligned landlord and tenant incentives.

summary.summary_250: It also says financing tools matter less than simple implementation support and trusted local contractors.
summary.summary_1000: It also says financing tools matter less than simple implementation support and trusted local contractors who reduce friction during the retrofit process.
summary.tldr: Trusted contractors and implementation support reduce retrofit friction.

summary.summary_250: The essay says retrofit adoption improves when incentives are aligned and implementation support is simple enough for building owners to trust.
summary.summary_1000: The essay says retrofit adoption improves when landlord and tenant incentives are aligned, avoiding the usual split-benefit problem. It also argues that trusted local contractors and low-friction implementation support matter more than financing complexity for getting projects across the line.
summary.tldr: Aligned incentives plus low-friction implementation support make retrofits more likely.
